text: The robotic surgical system is docked on the patient's left side, with the camera view showing proximity to the left linea alba. The surgeon notes potential visibility challenges of the linea alba during the procedure.
The surgeon uses their left hand to pull the suture while simultaneously pushing with the back of the needle driver, efficiently advancing the suture through the tissue.
The surgeon pushes the suture to one side while the bedside assistant palpates the skin, revealing the thin subcutaneous tissue layer.

The surgeon lays out the suture without tightening it, ensuring the entire strand is used before returning to tighten it. The thick subcutaneous tissue prevents the patient from feeling the instruments, allowing the surgeon to comfortably grasp the center part of the sac.

The surgeon utilizes the width of the defect to efficiently manage the length of the 24-inch suture, ensuring it does not feel cumbersome. After tightening the suture, additional length is gained, allowing for further suturing.
The surgeon lays out multiple sutures across the defect and progressively tensions each one, distributing the tension evenly along the length of the defect to avoid entanglement and knot formation.
The surgeon pulls together large defects at the caudal end, demonstrating the technique used to approximate the tissue edges.

The surgeon tightens the suture at the edges of the linea alba, where the defect is narrow. As the procedure progresses to the wider portion of the defect, the suture is laid out across without immediate tightening.
The surgeon uses multiple suture bites on the fascia to progressively tighten and bring the edges of the linea alba together.
The surgeon reaches the wider portion of the defect and requests a new twenty-four inch suture, preparing to advance the suturing process.

The surgeon aligns the edges of the linea alba up to the suture, initially taking bites that avoid the sac. Subsequent sutures incorporate small bites of the sac to prevent the formation of a large seroma.
The surgeon uses a 24-inch suture to progressively tension the tissue, which reduces a single large seroma into multiple smaller seromas. The suture is quickly consumed as the defect is left open.
The surgeon encounters difficulty visualizing the linea alba as the defect is closed, with the rectus muscle visible as a vertical structure extending from the costal margin to the pubis.

The surgeon observes the medialization of the rectus muscle and the retraction of the linea alba relative to the muscle, highlighting the visual changes from this vantage point during the progressive tensioning method.
The surgeon lays out sutures across the widest part of the defect and the area where the linea alba could disappear, ensuring visibility of the linea alba before tightening the defect.
The surgeon reaches the upper cephalad portion of the widest part of the defect, using the remaining suture to tighten the area without introducing additional sutures.

The surgeon leaves the needle in place and uses a technique to pull the suture with the right hand perpendicular to the abdominal wall while the left hand braces the tissue. This method helps gauge tension effectively, preventing suture breakage and avoiding damage to the fascia or muscle.

The surgeon observes that the suture strands are now looser compared to earlier, due to the tightening of downstream sutures which have brought the fascial edges closer together, facilitating easier re-approximation of the defect.

The surgeon uses the extra suture length to finish suturing the re-approximated linea alba between the first and second strand of suture, gently bringing the tissue together without applying excessive tension.

The surgeon reduces the pneumoperitoneum pressure to facilitate instrument reach, parking the needle before tightening the next suture strand to maintain organization and prevent confusion between strands.
The surgeon approaches the cranial portion of the defect, carefully managing the suture strands to prevent confusion about needle ownership during tightening.

The surgeon identifies small holes and weak pockets in the fascia and begins re-approximating these areas. The re-approximation stops where the widest part of the linea alba tapers to a more normal diastasis, without tracing all the way up to the xiphoid.

The surgeon revisits earlier suture strands, adjusting them as they become looser while moving from caudal to cranial and back to caudal, demonstrating the technique used to close larger defects. The same principle is applied to smaller defects, such as a three or four centimeter umbilical hernia, with sutures laid out similarly.

The surgeon demonstrates the technique of progressive tensioning by laying out a longer suture across the entire length of the defect. The needle is visibly parked to maintain clarity on which strand belongs to which needle, preventing confusion during the tightening process.

The surgeon manages multiple strands of suture by walking back the initial two sutures, cutting them, and pushing them aside to prevent entanglement with the third suture while working on the cranial part of the defect.
The surgeon takes bites on the posterior rectus sheath edge, occasionally walking the suture back to lock it with a few throws.
The surgeon positions a mesh with approximately four centimeters of overlap over the defect areas to prevent future hernia formation.
The surgeon dissects and divides more of the posterior rectus sheath to achieve four centimeters of mesh overlap, addressing the bulge in the abdominal wall contour.

The surgeon employs a systematic approach to defect closure using a progressive tensioning method with long strands of V-lock suture. The strands are pulled perpendicular to the tissue while bracing with the left hand. The surgeon checks the appearance of the abdominal wall at the bedside to ensure the bulge is resolved before finishing the procedure.
